draft.introduction: Mencius 1A:7 records a dialogue with King Xuan of Qi in which Mencius argues that the king already possesses the compassion needed for benevolent rule—he merely needs to extend it from the ox he pitied to the people he governs.

translation.text: Mencius said to King Xuan of Qi: "I have heard that Your Majesty, upon seeing an ox being led to sacrifice, could not bear its trembling and ordered a sheep to replace it. Is this true?" The King said: "It is." Mencius said: "This compassion is sufficient to become a true king. The people all thought you were being stingy, but I know that you simply could not bear the suffering of the creature before your eyes. If you can extend this compassion from the ox to the people, your kingdom will flourish."
translation.original_text: 王说曰：诗云他人有心，予忱度之

translator_notes.note: This passage illustrates Mencius's method of moral education: he does not lecture the king about abstract principles but identifies a concrete moment of compassion the king has already experienced. The task is not to create virtue from nothing but to extend (tui) the innate moral feelings one already possesses to wider and wider circles of concern.
